# infra-loop-timing — REPORT

*Generated by `timing_extract.py` at 2026-06-28T15:30:10.054410.*
*Sources used: local.*

## What this measures

Per-stage wall-clock decomposition of the YOLO tick-tock loop, built from timing data we already emit — **no new instrumentation**:

- **Local council JSON timestamps** (offline): inter-gate intervals for a single build. NOTE: each interval conflates *build/fix time* with *council latency* — it is not council time alone.
- **GitHub Actions step durations** (CI-only, via `gh ... --json jobs`): the non-AI stages (checkout, pip/npm install, git push) the council JSON cannot see.

### Data-source decision

We use both *existing* sources and instrument nothing. Instrumenting `council.py` would measure only council latency and miss the non-AI stages the hypothesis targets, while adding code to the hot path. GHA step boundaries + council JSON timestamps cover the full loop for free. The gh path reads `--json jobs` (structured step names), never parses workflow YAML, so a YAML reformat cannot break it.

## AI stages — council inter-gate intervals (local)

Coherent same-session builds analyzed: **11** (of 20 project dirs with council JSON; incoherent dirs hold gate files overwritten across different builds and are excluded from intervals).

> **Self-measurement note:** this tool reads council JSON, so its own build (`infra-loop-timing`) appears in the corpus once its gates run. Per-interval `n` can therefore differ by one across rows — the last gate (`tests->outcome`) is written after report generation, so it lags the earlier intervals by one build. This is expected, not a data bug.

| Interval | Median (s) | Max (s) | n |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| plan->implementation | 247.5 | 1308.1 | 11 |
| implementation->tests | 79.4 | 11526.1 | 11 |
| tests->outcome | 49.0 | 1486.6 | 11 |

Advisory objections by gate (across coherent builds): `tests`=9, `implementation`=8, `plan`=7, `outcome`=7.

## Non-AI stages — GitHub Actions step durations

_No live GitHub Actions data (no `GH_TOKEN` in this environment)._ The gh-mode test against ≥5 real recent runs runs in CI, where the runner has `GH_TOKEN`. Invoke there with `python3 timing_extract.py --source gh --runs 5 --report`.

## Bottlenecks identified

- **[non_ai / high]** Install steps run UNCACHED on every invocation (no actions/cache and no setup-* cache: key): pip install anthropic requests google-generativeai; npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
  - *Fix:* Add actions/cache (or setup-python/node cache:) for pip + npm so install steps are near-instant on warm cache.
- **[ai / info]** Slowest AI inter-gate interval (median): plan->implementation = 247.5s (build+fix+council, n=11)
  - *Fix:* AI work dominates here; reduce escalation rounds rather than raw model speed.

## Verdict on the hypothesis

The decomposition separates **non-AI mechanical overhead** (uncached installs, checkout, push — fixed cost every hourly run, independent of build difficulty) from **AI stages** (inter-gate build/fix/council intervals). The mechanical overhead is the bottleneck class that is *directly fixable without faster models* — caching dependencies removes it from every run. AI inter-gate time is dominated by escalation/fix rounds, not raw model latency, so the lever there is fewer rounds, not a faster backbone.
